2022 · TMLR · Benchmarks & Evals

Beyond the Imitation Game: Quantifying and Extrapolating the Capabilities of Language Models

A 204-task benchmark, assembled by 450 authors across 132 institutions and deliberately stocked with problems believed to be beyond current models. The point is not to crown a winner but to chart what language models can't yet do and forecast what scale will unlock: even 2022's largest models score under 20 on a 0–100 human-normalized scale, while expert raters approach 100.

♪ listen — audio episode

Aarohi Srivastava, Abhinav Rastogi, Abhishek Rao, … (450 authors, 132 institutions)
venue TMLR citations 2,559 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By 2022 the field's benchmarks had a forecasting problem. Most measured a narrow slice of ability — language understanding, summarization, trivia QA — and usually a slice on which models were already known to do well. That makes them blind to the thing that actually matters as models scale: new and unexpected capabilities that did not exist at the previous size. Worse, the good benchmarks had short useful lifespans. SuperGLUE reached its human baseline within roughly two years of release, triggering a "challenge-solve-and-replace" treadmill where each saturated benchmark is swapped for a slightly harder one.

The deeper issue is illustrated by the second panel below. If you plot a model family's accuracy across existing benchmarks against scale, you get a clean, near-log-linear trend that extrapolates smoothly toward human performance. That smoothness is seductive but uninformative: it tells you the average is creeping up, while saying nothing about which specific capability appears at which scale, or where a sudden jump is waiting. To "prepare for disruptive new model capabilities, and ameliorate socially harmful effects," the authors argue, you need a single benchmark that is broad, forward-looking, and deliberately populated with tasks current models fail — a benchmark designed to stay unsaturated for years.

// how it works

The method

BIG-bench is an open, collaboratively built benchmark: tasks were submitted through GitHub under a peer-review process, landing at 204 tasks from 450 authors across 132 institutions. Topics deliberately span far past NLP's comfort zone — linguistics, childhood development, math, common-sense reasoning, biology, physics, software development, social bias, and beyond — with the explicit instruction to favor problems thought to be beyond current models. Most tasks are JSON tasks: declarative lists of input/target pairs that grade automatically (multiple-choice via conditional log-likelihood, or generative via exact/fuzzy string match). A smaller set are programmatic Python tasks that interact with the model over multiple turns.

Every task ships its own preferred metric plus author-specified low and high anchors, so heterogeneous scores can be pooled. The aggregate "normalized preferred metric" linearly rescales each raw score so that 0 is poor and 100 is excellent, then averages across tasks:

normalized = 100 × raw − lowhigh − low // per-task rescaling; a model can score <0 (worse than random) or >100. Experts ≈ 100.

Four design choices give the benchmark its teeth. (1) A cheap subset — BIG-bench Lite (24 tasks) — lets researchers evaluate without running all 204. (2) Many model classes and sizes: OpenAI's GPT, Google's dense "BIG-G" transformers, Switch-style sparse "BIG-G sparse" transformers, and PaLM, sweeping from millions to hundreds of billions of parameters at 0–3 shots. (3) A human-expert baseline: a team of raters completed the tasks themselves — encouraged to use any resource, internet search included — reporting both mean and max rater scores. (4) A contamination guard: every task file embeds a canary GUID string (26b5c67b-…) so future crawls can filter BIG-bench out of training data, and a dedicated training_on_test_set task probes, after the fact, whether a model was trained on the benchmark.

// what they found

The results

Scale helps, but the absolute level is low. Aggregate normalized performance rises with both compute and number of shots — yet the best models scored less than 20 on the 0–100 scale where experts approach 100. On the human-rated tasks (Figure 1a) the best rater sits near ≈78 and the average rater near ≈44, with the largest models around ≈14; on BIG-bench Lite (Figure 1c) the best rater is ≈80, the average rater ≈33, and the strongest model (PaLM) reaches only ≈42. The gap between frontier models and human experts is the headline, and it is large.

Calibration improves with scale but stays poor. On the 109 multiple-choice JSON tasks, larger models assign more honest confidences — yet Brier scores stay around 0.2–0.3 and expected calibration error around 0.25–0.45, i.e. still meaningfully overconfident. Loss is lawful even when capability isn't: per-token cross-entropy follows a clean power law (exponent ≈ −0.066 for GPT, −0.068 for BIG-G, −0.069 for BIG-G sparse) that is nearly identical across model classes — but that smooth loss curve does not translate into smooth downstream task scores.

Two scaling shapes — and a warning about both. The authors define heuristic linearity and breakthroughness scores. Tasks that improve gradually and predictably ("linear") tend to lean on knowledge or memorization; tasks that jump suddenly at a critical scale ("breakthrough") tend to require multiple reasoning steps — or to use a brittle metric. The emoji_movie task makes the warning concrete (Figure 9): only the 128B model decodes 👧🐟🐠🐡 as Finding Nemo, and under exact-string-match the ability looks like an abrupt breakthrough, while under multiple-choice grading the very same runs look gradual. "Emergence" is partly in the eye of the metric.

FindingMeasureValue
Best model, aggregatenormalized preferred metric (0–100)< 20
Human raters (human-eval tasks)best / average rater, Fig. 1a≈78 / ≈44
CalibrationBrier / ECE, 109 MC JSON tasks0.2–0.3 / 0.25–0.45
Cross-entropy scalingpower-law exponent (GPT / BIG-G / sparse)−0.066 / −0.068 / −0.069

Models are brittle, and bias tracks scale. Performance swings with surface phrasing: on cause_and_effect, models sit at chance when asked explicitly which event is the cause, yet implicitly judge causation correctly by assigning higher probability to the better-ordered sentence. And social bias increases with scale in broad or ambiguous contexts, decreases with scale in narrow unambiguous ones, and can be partially steered by prompting — a result that complicates any "bigger is safer" story.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

BIG-bench's lasting contribution is methodological — breadth, a forward-looking task philosophy, the canary contamination guard, and the linearity/breakthroughness lens — and those have aged well. The numbers, predictably, have not: the evaluated models are a 2022 snapshot (GPT-3-era dense models and PaLM 540B), so the "best model < 20" headline says nothing about frontier systems three years on. Read it as a protocol, not a leaderboard.

The benchmark's signature strength — 204 crowd-sourced tasks — is also its softest spot. Task quality, difficulty calibration, and size vary enormously (the size histogram is heavy-tailed, and some tasks are tiny), yet every task counts equally in the aggregate after an author-chosen low/high rescaling. That makes the single normalized number a blunt instrument: it can move because a few brittle-metric tasks flip, not because broad capability changed — exactly the emoji_movie failure mode the paper itself surfaces. The human baseline is honest about its own limits: raters had internet access (so it is an assisted-human ceiling, not unaided), and the authors explicitly call collapsing wildly different per-task expertise into one "human performance" number a "fraught enterprise."

On contamination, the canary string is a genuinely good idea but a forward-looking one: it can keep BIG-bench out of corpora scraped after it shipped, but cannot retract data already crawled, and it depends on every downstream paper dutifully echoing the GUID. The training_on_test_set task is only a partial, post-hoc check — and later work on search-time and web-scale contamination (which cites BIG-bench) shows how leaky this remains in practice.

baselines

Many model families (GPT, BIG-G dense, BIG-G sparse, PaLM) across a wide scale sweep — strong for 2022. But the human baseline is internet-assisted and self-describedly hard to reduce to one number; it is a soft ceiling, not a clean one.

contamination

A canary GUID + training_on_test_set probe is forward-looking and depends on downstream compliance; it cannot un-leak already-scraped data. Genuinely ahead of its time, still incomplete.

what's missed

The 0–100 aggregate hides per-task heterogeneity and rides on author-set anchors and metric choices. "Breakthroughs" can be metric artifacts; equal-weighting 204 uneven tasks blurs what actually changed.

would raise confidence

Per-task quality/difficulty vetting, adversarially-filtered held-out subsets, consistent (e.g. judge-based) grading, and re-running the whole suite on current frontier models to refresh the now-obsolete absolute scores.

// bottom line

A landmark not because of any single score but because it reframed evaluation as forecasting: stock a benchmark with what models can't do, sweep scale, and watch which capabilities switch on. Its 2022 numbers are stale, and its crowd-sourced aggregate is coarse — but the breadth, the canary guard, and the breakthrough/linearity vocabulary it gave the field still organize how we argue about emergence today.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Evaluation as forecasting. BIG-bench's move was to fill 204 tasks with problems models fail, then read capability off the scaling curve. Loss scales smoothly (power-law exponent ≈ −0.066 to −0.069, near-identical across model classes), but downstream capability does not — that mismatch is the whole story.
  2. Emergence is partly a metric artifact. The emoji_movie task looks like an abrupt breakthrough under exact-match and a gentle slope under multiple-choice grade — the same runs, two stories. This tension seeded the later Emergent Abilities claim (which cites BIG-bench) and the "mirage" rebuttals.
  3. Scale ≠ safety, and bigger ≠ trustworthy. Even the largest 2022 models scored <20 vs ~100 for experts, stayed poorly calibrated (Brier 0.2–0.3, ECE 0.25–0.45), were brittle to phrasing, and grew more biased with scale in ambiguous contexts. The canary-string contamination guard and the 0–100 human-normalized aggregate it popularized are now standard practice.
